items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: Why has the Richter scale been largely replaced by the moment magnitude scale?
items.correct: Richter saturates above magnitude ~7 and was calibrated for local Californian quakes; moment magnitude (Mw) measures the total energy released and works for all sizes globally.
items.misconception: The Richter scale is still the standard scale used by all seismologists.
items.random_wrong: Moment magnitude measures damage while Richter measures shaking.
items.why: Media still says 'Richter' but seismologists now report Mw. Both are logarithmic — a magnitude 7 releases ~32x more energy than a 6 — but Mw is the modern standard for large quakes.

items.q: Why is 'renewable' not the same as 'clean' energy?
items.correct: Renewable means the source replenishes naturally on human timescales; clean means low environmental/health impact. Hydropower is renewable but floods habitats; bioenergy is renewable but can drive deforestation.
items.misconception: All renewable energy sources are clean and have no environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Clean energy means it doesn't produce any smoke when burned.
items.why: Large dams cause major ecological and social harm despite being renewable. Nuclear is low-carbon but not renewable. The two categories overlap but don't match — important for energy-policy evaluation.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: Correct answer: Sahel. “Maghreb” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: It's 250mm. “100mm” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
